stem: A 38-year-old client with full-thickness circumferential burns to the left arm is showing signs of compromised circulation. Based on the client's electronic health record, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions the nurse should take, and two parameters to monitor.

physicalExam.general: Anxious male in moderate distress. Alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: HR 128 bpm, regular. Radial pulse absent to palpation on the left, weak Doppler signal. Capillary refill >5 seconds on the left hand.
physicalExam.respiratory: RR 24 breaths/min, unlabored. SpO2 96% on room air. Breath sounds clear bilaterally.
physicalExam.integumentary: Full-thickness circumferential burn to the left arm. Eschar is tight and non-compliant. Coolness and pallor noted distal to the burn.
physicalExam.neurological: Unable to flex fingers on the left hand. Reports numbness and tingling in the left hand.

clinicalData.radiology: X-Ray Left Arm: Soft tissue swelling noted circumferentially around the left arm. No acute bony abnormalities identified. Doppler ultrasound shows reduced arterial flow distal to the elbow.
nursingOrders: Prepare for and assist with escharotomy.
Maintain sterile technique during wound care.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: At 14:30, I'm calling about Jameson Reynolds, a 38-year-old male with a full-thickness circumferential left arm burn. He is showing clear signs of acute limb ischemia, reporting severe pain and anxiety.

Background: The burn occurred two hours ago from an industrial explosion. He has no significant medical history and has received no medications.

Assessment: The left arm eschar is tight and constricting. His hand is cool and pale with capillary refill >5 seconds. The radial pulse is absent by palpation, with only a weak Doppler signal. He rates his pain as 8/10.

Recommendation: I request an immediate evaluation for an emergent escharotomy. We will continue hourly neurovascular checks and prepare the escharotomy tray.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content: BP 132/84 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 24 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. The client reports severe pain (8/10).

tabs.title: Perfusion & Tissue Status
tabs.content:
Assessment ItemCurrent FindingInterpretation
Distal Pulse (Radial)Absent to palpation; Weak Doppler 'hiss'Severe Obstruction
Skin ConsistencyEschar is non-compliant and restrictiveLimb constriction
Capillary Refill> 5 secondsPoor perfusion
MovementInability to flex fingersNerve/Muscle ischemia

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: X-Ray Left Arm: Soft tissue swelling noted circumferentially around the left arm. No acute bony abnormalities identified. Doppler ultrasound shows reduced arterial flow distal to the elbow.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content: Lactate: 3.8 mmol/L (Elevated). Creatine Kinase (CK): 250 U/L (Slightly Elevated). Other labs pending.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Priority Nursing Diagnoses: Risk for Peripheral Neurovascular Dysfunction related to circumferential burn and edema. Acute Pain related to tissue ischemia and burn injury. Risk for Infection related to skin barrier disruption.

Interventions: Prepare for and assist with escharotomy. Perform hourly neurovascular assessments. Administer pain medication as prescribed. Maintain sterile technique during wound care. Elevate affected extremity.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Morphine Sulfate 4mg IV PRN for pain. Tetanus Toxoid 0.5mL IM as per burn protocol.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Anxious male in moderate distress. Alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Cardiovascular: HR 128 bpm, regular. Radial pulse absent to palpation on the left, weak Doppler signal. Capillary refill >5 seconds on the left hand.

Respiratory: RR 24 breaths/min, unlabored. SpO2 96% on room air. Breath sounds clear bilaterally.

Integumentary: Full-thickness circumferential burn to the left arm. Eschar is tight and non-compliant. Coolness and pallor noted distal to the burn.

Neurological: Unable to flex fingers on the left hand. Reports numbness and tingling in the left hand.

conditionOptions.hoverRationale: This is incorrect. DVT involves venous clot formation, causing unilateral swelling, warmth, and redness. This client's signs of pallor, coolness, and an absent arterial pulse indicate arterial occlusion.

This is correct. Full-thickness burns create inelastic eschar. As underlying tissue swells, the eschar acts as a tourniquet, compressing blood vessels and leading to limb-threatening compartment syndrome.

This is incorrect. While inflammation is expected, the complete loss of a palpable pulse and severe neurovascular compromise are not. This represents a pathological progression requiring urgent intervention.

actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is correct. Escharotomy is the definitive treatment to release the constricting eschar, decompress the underlying tissue, and restore distal blood flow in burn-induced compartment syndrome.

actionOptions.text: Use a Doppler ultrasound to reassess pulses
actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is correct. Frequent neurovascular assessment is critical. A Doppler is necessary when pulses are not palpable, providing a more sensitive measure of blood flow to monitor the progression of ischemia.
This is incorrect. Applying bandages would further increase external pressure on the compromised limb, worsening the ischemia. This action is contraindicated.

actionOptions.text: Place the arm in a dependent position
actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is incorrect. Placing the arm in a dependent position can increase edema, which would worsen the compression. The limb should be elevated at or slightly above the level of the heart.

parameterOptions.hoverRationale: This is correct. Monitoring for the return of pulses, improved color, warmth, and sensation are direct indicators of reperfusion and the effectiveness of interventions for limb ischemia.
This is correct. The client was in an industrial explosion, which carries a high risk of inhalation injury. Monitoring respiratory effort is a critical safety priority for any major burn patient.

This is incorrect. While burn patients can experience hyperglycemia due to stress, monitoring fasting glucose is not an immediate priority for managing acute limb ischemia. Neurovascular status is paramount.

parameterOptions.text: Presence of a positive Babinski sign
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: This is incorrect. The Babinski sign assesses for an upper motor neuron lesion in the central nervous system and is irrelevant to assessing peripheral neurovascular compromise in a burned extremity.

pearls.description: Escharotomy is a critical intervention for circumferential burns causing compartment syndrome, relieving pressure and restoring blood flow.
pearls.strategy: Recognize early signs of compartment syndrome (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis) and advocate for prompt surgical intervention.

pearls.description: Inhalation injuries are common in burn victims from explosions. Continuous respiratory monitoring is essential.
pearls.strategy: Assess for signs of respiratory distress (stridor, wheezing, dyspnea) and be prepared to assist with airway management.

traps.description: Applying constricting bandages to a limb with compromised circulation will worsen ischemia.
traps.strategy: Avoid any interventions that increase pressure on the affected limb. Elevate the limb to minimize edema, but do not constrict it.

traps.description: Delaying escharotomy can lead to irreversible tissue damage and limb loss.
traps.strategy: Act quickly upon recognizing signs of compartment syndrome. Time is critical in restoring perfusion and preventing long-term complications.

mnemonics.description: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pressure
Breathing, Urine output, Resuscitation, Neurovascular assessment

answerBreakdown.c1: Incorrect: DVT involves venous clot formation, causing unilateral swelling, warmth, and redness. This client's signs of pallor, coolness, and an absent arterial pulse indicate arterial occlusion.
answerBreakdown.c2: Correct: Full-thickness burns create inelastic eschar. As underlying tissue swells, the eschar acts as a tourniquet, compressing blood vessels and leading to limb-threatening compartment syndrome.
answerBreakdown.c3: Incorrect: While inflammation is expected, the complete loss of a palpable pulse and severe neurovascular compromise are not. This represents a pathological progression requiring urgent intervention.
answerBreakdown.a1: Correct: Escharotomy is the definitive treatment to release the constricting eschar, decompress the underlying tissue, and restore distal blood flow in burn-induced compartment syndrome.
answerBreakdown.a2: Correct: Frequent neurovascular assessment is critical. A Doppler is necessary when pulses are not palpable, providing a more sensitive measure of blood flow to monitor the progression of ischemia.
answerBreakdown.a3: Incorrect: Applying bandages would further increase external pressure on the compromised limb, worsening the ischemia. This action is contraindicated.
answerBreakdown.a4: Incorrect: Placing the arm in a dependent position can increase edema, which would worsen the compression. The limb should be elevated at or slightly above the level of the heart.
answerBreakdown.p1: Correct: Monitoring for the return of pulses, improved color, warmth, and sensation are direct indicators of reperfusion and the effectiveness of interventions for limb ischemia.
answerBreakdown.p2: Correct: The client was in an industrial explosion, which carries a high risk of inhalation injury. Monitoring respiratory effort is a critical safety priority for any major burn patient.
answerBreakdown.p3: Incorrect: While burn patients can experience hyperglycemia due to stress, monitoring fasting glucose is not an immediate priority for managing acute limb ischemia. Neurovascular status is paramount.
answerBreakdown.p4: Incorrect: The Babinski sign assesses for an upper motor neuron lesion in the central nervous system and is irrelevant to assessing peripheral neurovascular compromise in a burned extremity.
